title: Spatially Adaptive Detection for Satellite-based QKD under Atmospheric Turbulence Channel
authors: Yaoxuan Yang, Ivi Afxenti, Majid Safari
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) provides information-theoretic security and satellite-based quantum key distribution (SatQKD) has demonstrated the potential to extend this communication security to intercontinental scales. However, atmospheric turbulence induces significant distortion in the spatial distribution of received optical beams, while background noise remains approximately uniform across the detector plane. As a result, single-element qubit (quantum bit) detection can be frequently dominated by noise due to the random spatial pattern of the imaged wavefront, thereby degrading the system performance. To address this limitation, we propose to exploit the spatial degrees of freedom of single-photon detector arrays to reject the excessive noise while adapting to channel variations induced by turbulence. We develop a threshold-based selection method that only activates detector elements that have higher probability of registering qubits. We evaluate the performance of the proposed noise-rejection QKD schemes using Monte Carlo simulations considering the impact of diffraction and atmospheric turbulence on the transmitted optical beam in the presence of background and dark noise. The results show that, compared to conventional schemes, the proposed noise-rejection strategy effectively reduces the quantum bit error rate (QBER) and improves the secret key rate (SKR) performance, while the performance gains depend on the turbulence condition. These findings demonstrate the potential of adaptive array receiver design to enhance the robustness of the SatQKD system under realistic atmospheric conditions.
summary: The paper proposes an adaptive receiver design for satellite-based quantum key distribution that uses detector arrays to filter out noise-heavy regions caused by atmospheric turbulence. By selectively activating only the most promising detector elements, the system significantly improves the secret key rate. This approach is particularly effective in moderate turbulence conditions where spatial signal fluctuations are most pronounced.
main_problem: Atmospheric turbulence in satellite-to-ground QKD causes spatial beam distortion and intensity fluctuations, leading to high background noise interference in single-element detectors.
main_result: A spatially adaptive detector activation strategy effectively reduces the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) and improves the Secret Key Rate (SKR), with the highest performance gains observed in moderate turbulence regimes.
method: The authors propose a threshold-based selection method and a 'Best-K' strategy to selectively activate detector elements with high signal probability, evaluated using Monte Carlo simulations and the Split-Step Fourier Method.
model_or_system: A satellite-to-ground downlink QKD system using an 8x8 Single Photon Detector (SPD) array at 850 nm, modeling a 1200 km link with atmospheric turbulence via the Hufnagel-Valley model and Kolmogorov statistics.
key_observables: Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), Secret Key Rate (SKR), Sifted Bit Rate (SBR), and Error Bit Rate (EBR).
important_parameters: Turbulence strength (weak, moderate, strong), noise level (background and dark counts), focal length, and detector array size.
main_assumptions: The channel is quasi-static within each clock period, the transmitter is an ideal single-photon source, and background noise is spatially uniform.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows statistical model fitting (Gamma, lognormal, Gamma-Gamma) to intensity distributions; Fig 2 illustrates focal-plane speckle patterns under different turbulence and focal lengths; Fig 3 compares QBER and SKR for different strategies across turbulence regimes.
paper_structure: Introduction of the SatQKD problem, description of the physical system and turbulence model, presentation of the proposed adaptive detection strategies, statistical analysis of intensity distributions, performance evaluation via Monte Carlo simulations, and discussion of results and limitations.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it addresses the practical challenge of maintaining entanglement/signal integrity in free-space quantum communication channels subject to decoherence-like atmospheric effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Statistical characterization of focal-plane intensity at different detector positions. (a) Corner element, (b) edge element, and (c) center element show the histogram fitting results. The horizontal axis represents the received intensity, and the vertical axis represents the occurrence frequency (histogram counts). (d) Schematic illustration of detector positions within the array.

Fig. 2. Example realizations of focal-plane speckle intensity distributions under different turbulence conditions. (a) Weak, (b) moderate, and (c) strong turbulence for a focal length of f = 0.5 m, and (d) weak turbulence with an increased focal length of f = 4 m. The horizontal and vertical axes represent spatial coordinates on the focal plane, and the color scale indicates intensity. The white grid denotes the detector array layout.

Fig. 3. Performance comparison of different detector-selection strategies under different turbulence conditions. (a) QBER and (b) SKR under weak turbulence; (c) QBER and (d) SKR under moderate turbulence; (e) QBER and (f) SKR under strong turbulence. The horizontal axis represents the noise level nB, and the vertical axis denotes QBER or SKR (Hz), respectively.
